title: Cakey brownies
subtitle: Chocolate traybake with a light, set crumb
excerpt: Brownies baked through to a clean set: firm enough to slice, with a tender chocolate crumb rather than a fudgy centre. More flour and a longer bake than fudgy brownies. The top sets to a thin, crackled crust.
sourceNotes: Brownies are an American invention documented from the early 20th century; the first printed brownie recipe appears in the 1906 Boston Cooking-School Cook Book by Fannie Farmer. The distinction between fudgy and cakey brownies comes down to flour ratio and bake time. The cakey version, with more flour and a fully set centre, is closer to the earliest published recipes.

content.text: Cakey brownies are distinguished from fudgy ones by a higher flour ratio and a longer bake. They cut cleanly, hold their shape, and have a tender chocolate crumb rather than a dense centre. The
is precise; check at 25 minutes and again at 30.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease and line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Melt the chocolate and butter together in a saucepan over low heat, stirring until smooth. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Whisk in the sugar, then add the eggs one at a time, whisking well after each. Add the vanilla. Sift over the flour and salt and fold in until just combined.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and spread level. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with moist crumbs rather than wet batter, and the top has formed a thin crackled crust. Leave to cool completely in the tin before cutting into 16 squares.
